questions.question: What are Lithospheric plates?
questions.correct_answer: Large and small, rigid, irregularly shaped plates which carry continents and the ocean floor
questions.explanation: The earth's crust is broken into these plates which move very slowly—just a few millimeters each year.

questions.question: What causes the movement of Lithospheric plates?
questions.correct_answer: The movement of the molten magma inside the earth
questions.explanation: The molten magma inside the earth moves in a circular manner, acting as a driving force for the plates.

questions.question: Differentiate between Endogenic and Exogenic forces.
questions.correct_answer: Endogenic forces act in the interior of the earth; Exogenic forces act on the surface
questions.explanation: Endogenic forces cause earthquakes and volcanoes; Exogenic forces cause weathering and erosion.

questions.question: Define 'Focus' and 'Epicentre' of an earthquake.
questions.correct_answer: Focus is where the movement starts; Epicentre is the place on the surface above the focus
questions.explanation: Vibrations travel outwards from the epicentre as waves; greatest damage usually occurs closest to the epicentre.

questions.question: What is a 'Meander'?
questions.correct_answer: Large bends or loops formed by a river as it enters a plain
questions.explanation: Continuous erosion and deposition along the sides of the meander lead to the formation of ox-bow lakes.

questions.question: How is an 'Ox-bow lake' formed?
questions.correct_answer: When a meander loop is cut off from the main river due to continuous erosion and deposition
questions.explanation: As the loop cuts off, it forms a separate crescent-shaped lake called an ox-bow lake.

questions.question: What are 'Sand dunes'?
questions.correct_answer: Low hill-like structures formed by the deposition of sand in deserts
questions.explanation: When the wind blows, it lifts and transports sand; when it stops, the sand falls and gets deposited in these mounds.

questions.question: What is a 'Glacial Moraine'?
questions.correct_answer: Accumulation of rocks, sand, and silt deposited by a glacier
questions.explanation: As glaciers melt, the material they carry is left behind, forming these distinct ridges.

questions.question: How are 'Sea caves' and 'Stacks' formed?
questions.correct_answer: Sea waves strike rocks, forming hollow caves; eventually, only the walls are left (stacks)
questions.explanation: Continuous erosion turns cracks into caves, then arches, and finally just walls called stacks.

questions.question: What instrument is used to measure an earthquake?
questions.explanation: The magnitude of the earthquake is measured on the Richter scale.

notes.title: Earthquake Magnitude
notes.content: An earthquake of 2.0 or less is felt only a little; 5.0 causes damage; 7.0 is classified as a major earthquake.

notes.title: Weathering vs Erosion
notes.content: Weathering is the breaking of rocks; Erosion is the wearing away of the landscape by water, wind, and ice.

notes.title: Mushroom Rocks
notes.content: Winds erode the lower section of the rock more than the upper part, giving it a mushroom-like shape.

facts.fact: Angel Falls of Venezuela in South America is the highest waterfall in the world.
Victoria Falls is located on the borders of Zambia and Zimbabwe in Africa.
When very fine sand is deposited in large areas, it is called Loess; large deposits are found in China.
